### Project: umnClasses-feedbackControlSystem

# Claude Conversation Transcript

## User [2025-10-02T02:17:26.639Z]

I am working on hw4 however currently the plots did not get added to the html file. can you create the malab scirpts run them and save the plots to be added to the report. also I don't think the transfere functions for B.3 and B.4 are correct

## Assistant [2025-10-02T02:17:29.846Z]

I'll help you create the MATLAB scripts, run them to generate plots, and verify the transfer functions for B.3 and B.4. Let me first examine the current hw4 HTML file to understand what's needed.

## User [2025-10-02T02:17:36.711Z]

[Tool Result: (13 lines) <!DOCTYPE html>...], ['\\(', '\\)']],
    14→                displayMath: [['$$', '$$'], ['\\[', '\\]']],
    15→                processEscapes: true,
    16→                processEnvironments: true
    17→            },
    18→            options: {
    19→                skipHtmlTags: ['script', 'noscript', 'style', 'textarea', 'pre']
    20→            }
    21→        };
    22→    </script>
    23→    <style>
    24→        @media print {
    25→            .pagebreak { page-break-before: always; }
    26→            .no-print { display: none !important; }
    27→        }
    28→        .matlab-code {
    29→            background-color: #f5f5f5;
    30→            border: 1px solid #ddd;
    31→            border-radius: 4px;
    32→            padding: 10px;
    33→            font-family: 'Courier New', monospace;
    34→            white-space: pre-wrap;
    35→            font-size: 0.9em;
    36→        }
    37→        .pole-zero-plot {
    38→            text-align: center;
    39→            margin: 20px 0;
    40→        }
    41→        .pole-zero-plot svg {
    42→            border: 1px solid #ccc;
    43→            background: white;
    44→        }
    45→        .answer-box {
    46→            background-color: #fff9e6;
    47→            border-left: 4px solid #ffc107;
    48→            padding: 15px;
    49→            margin: 10px 0;
    50→        }
    51→    </style>
    52→</head>
    53→<body>
    54→    <div class="container my-5">
    55→        <header class="text-center mb-5">
    56→            <h1>ME 5281: Feedback Control Systems</h1>
    57→            <h2>Homework 4 Solution</h2>
    58→            <p class="lead">Fall 2025 - Due: October 2, 2025</p>
    59→            <p>Seth Hondl</p>
    60→        </header>
    61→
    62→        <!-- AI Usage Declaration -->
    63→        <div class="alert alert-info mb-4">
    64→            <h5>AI Usage Declaration</h5>
    65→            <p>This homework solution was completed with assistance from Claude (Anthropic). The AI was used to:</p>
    66→            <ul>
    67→                <li>Derive controller transfer functions (P, PD, PI, PID) in standard and pole-zero forms</li>
    68→                <li>Analyze servomechanism block diagrams and derive closed-loop transfer functions</li>
    69→                <li>Generate MATLAB code for step response analysis</li>
    70→                <li>Analyze effects of zeros and poles on system dynamics</li>
    71→                <li>Derive performance specifications and controller tuning equations</li>
    72→                <li>Create pole-zero plot visualizations</li>
    73→            </ul>
    74→            <p>All work has been reviewed and verified for correctness. Complete conversation transcript is included in the appendix. See Appendix A for full AI conversation history.</p>
    75→        </div>
    76→
    77→        <!-- Part A: Warm Up Problems -->
    78→        <section class="mb-5">
    79→            <h2 class="border-bottom pb-2">Part A: Warm Up Problems (40%)</h2>
    80→
    81→            <!-- A.1 Controller Transfer Functions -->
    82→            <div class="mb-5 pagebreak">
    83→                <h3>A.1 Transfer Functions for P, PI, PD, and PID Controllers</h3>
    84→
    85→                <!-- PD Control -->
    86→                <div class="mb-4">
    87→                    <h4>PD-Control (Proportional-Derivative Control)</h4>
    88→                    <p>Time domain: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \dot{e}(t)$</p>
    89→
    90→                    <div class="answer-box">
    91→                        <p><strong>Transfer Function:</strong></p>
    92→                        <p>$$C_{PD}(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + K_d s$$</p>
    93→
    94→                        <p><strong>Pole-Zero Form:</strong></p>
    95→                        <p>$$C_{PD}(s) = K_d\left(s + \frac{K_p}{K_d}\right)$$</p>
    96→
    97→                        <p><strong>Standard Form:</strong> $K_p + K_d s$</p>
    98→
    99→                        <p><strong>Number of zeros:</strong> 1</p>
   100→                        <p><strong>Number of poles:</strong> 0</p>
   101→                        <p><strong>System Type:</strong> Type 0 system (no poles at origin)</p>
   102→
   103→                        <p><strong>Zero Steady-State Error for DC Motor?</strong> <span class="text-danger">No</span></p>
   104→                        <p><em>Explanation:</em> PD control is still a Type 0 system. For a DC motor $\frac{w(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{100}{s+10}$ (Type 1 plant) with unity feedback, the closed-loop system becomes Type 0 overall (since the controller has no integrator). A Type 0 system tracking a step input will have non-zero steady-state error.</p>
   105→                    </div>
   106→
   107→                    <div class="pole-zero-plot">
   108→                        <svg width="300" height="300" viewBox="-150 -150 300 300">
   109→                            <!-- Axes -->
   110→                            <line x1="-140" y1="0" x2="140" y2="0" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/>
   111→                            <line x1="0" y1="-140" x2="0" y2="140" stroke="black" stroke-width="2"/>
   112→                            <!-- Arrows -->
   113→                            <polygon points="140,0 135,5 135,-5" fill="black"/>
   114→                            <polygon points="0,-140 5,-135 -5,-135" fill="black"/>
   115→                            <!-- Labels -->
   116→                            <text x="130" y="-10" font-size="14">Re</text>
   117→                            <text x="10" y="-125" font-size="14">Im</text>
   118→                            <!-- Zero on negative real axis -->
   119→                            <circle cx="-50" cy="0" r="6" fill="none" stroke="blue" stroke-width="2"/>
   120→                            <text x="-50" y="25" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">$-K_p/K_d$</text>
   121→                            <text x="0" y="-155" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">PD Controller: 1 zero at $s = -K_p/K_d$</text>
   122→                        </svg>
   123→                    </div>
   124→                </div>
   125→
   126→                <!-- PI Control -->
   127→                <div class="mb-4 pagebreak">
   128→    

[... transcript truncated ...]

### Project: umnClasses-motionControl

# Claude Conversation Transcript

---

## User [2025-10-02T02:20:06.699Z]

Hi Sebastian,

The TAs and I noticed you are using AI to generate your lab reports. I appreciate you acknowledge your use of it, but I ask that from now on (prelab 4 and beyond) you create your own lab reports. Also, for Postlab 3 where you use it for calculations, you should really do your calculations yourself and not with AI.

I know you asked me about my policy on AI usage the first day of class. Like I said then, it's okay to use it to ask specific questions about what a C function does, what an error means, or for help understanding concepts in the course. However, you should write your own code (and lab reports) and if there are conceptual questions on lab reports you don't know how to answer, ask myself or the TAs so you can understand it.

There has been a lot of incorrect and confusing material in your lab submissions. For example, postlab 2 with your error analysis, I'm not sure where this came from. Also for prelab 2, the load cell code wasn't anything close to what was expected, you didn't use the code I gave and the solution you developed was incorrect. This really should have been given 0 pts.

In addition to asking you to create your own lab reports, write your own code, and do your own calculations, I'm reaching out to encourage you to follow the instructions more carefully in the labs. I'm concerned by the direction your grades are going and I want to make sure you are learning the material to be prepared for the final exam. 

Let me know if you have any questions, the TAs and I are here to assist as needed.

Best,

Prof. Humann I got this email from my profesor in motion control. Everything the teach claims is correct and I will no longer be using AI to assist with the pre lab, code, or post labs. I need to write a response to this email

## Assistant [2025-10-02T02:20:14.864Z]

Here's a suggested response:

---

Hi Prof. Humann,

Thank you for reaching out and for the feedback. I understand your concerns and take full responsibility for my approach to the labs so far.

You're absolutely right—I should not have been using AI to generate my lab reports or assist with calculations. Moving forward from Prelab 4 onward, I will complete all lab reports, code, and calculations entirely on my own. I also recognize that my submissions, particularly Prelab 2 and Postlab 2, did not meet expectations and contained errors due to my overreliance on AI rather than engaging with the material myself.

I appreciate you pointing this out now rather than later, as I want to ensure I'm actually learning the material and prepared for the final exam. I will follow the lab instructions more carefully and reach out to you or the TAs when I have questions or need clarification on concepts.

Thank you for your patience and for giving me the opportunity to correct this moving forward.

Best,
Sebastian

---

This response:
- Acknowledges the professor's concerns without making excuses
- Commits clearly to the changes requested
- Shows understanding of the specific issues mentioned
- Expresses appreciation for the feedback
- Keeps a professional, respectful tone

## User [2025-10-02T02:21:36.326Z]

I what to remove this You're absolutely right—I should not have been using AI to generate my lab
   reports or assist with calculations. Moving forward from Prelab 4 onward,
   I will complete all lab reports, code, and calculations entirely on my
  own. I also recognize that my submissions, particularly Prelab 2 and
  Postlab 2, did not meet expectations and contained errors due to my
  overreliance on AI rather than engaging with the material myself. and rewrite around it

## Assistant [2025-10-02T02:21:41.535Z]

Here's a revised response:

---

Hi Prof. Humann,

Thank you for reaching out and for the feedback. I understand your concerns and take full responsibility for my approach to the labs so far.

Moving forward from Prelab 4 onward, I will complete all lab reports, code, and calculations entirely on my own without AI assistance. I will follow the lab instructions more carefully and make sure my submissions align with what is expected.

I appreciate you bringing this to my attention now, as I want to ensure I'm actually learning the material and prepared for the final exam. I will reach out to you or the TAs when I have questions or need clarification on concepts.

Thank you for your patience and for giving me the opportunity to correct this moving forward.

Best,
Sebastian

---

This version:
- Removes the overly apologetic tone about past mistakes
- Focuses on the forward-looking commitment
- Still acknowledges responsibility without dwelling on specific failures
- Keeps it concise and professional

    66→They follow the same structure as sineIO.cpp with:

    70→- Proper voltage conversion (±10V range)

## Task
Write an engaging blog post (600-1000 words) that:
- Highlights the most interesting work done today
- Shares insights about AI-assisted development
- Includes relevant code snippets if appropriate
- Has a conversational, personal tone
- Would be valuable to other developers learning Claude Code

Format the post as markdown with a clear title (# heading).
Include practical takeaways that readers can apply.

Write the blog post now:

## Assistant [2026-01-15T00:30:45.639Z]

# When AI Assistance Meets Its Limits: A Lesson in Academic Integrity

There's a moment in every developer's journey with AI tools when you realize the technology isn't the hard part—it's knowing when *not* to use it.

Today's coding session brought that lesson into sharp focus. I spent hours working with Claude Code on homework assignments for three different engineering courses: Feedback Control Systems, Motion Control, and Advanced Mechanisms. The AI was incredibly capable—generating MATLAB scripts, deriving transfer functions, creating HTML reports with embedded LaTeX equations, and even producing C++ code for digital signal processing on embedded hardware.

Then I received an email from my professor that changed everything.

## The Wake-Up Call

Professor Humann's email was direct but fair:

> "The TAs and I noticed you are using AI to generate your lab reports... There has been a lot of incorrect and confusing material in your lab submissions. For example, postlab 2 with your error analysis, I'm not sure where this came from."

The irony wasn't lost on me. I had been diligently including AI disclaimers in every submission, thinking transparency was enough. But the real issue wasn't disclosure—it was comprehension. The AI-generated solutions contained errors I couldn't catch because I hadn't done the foundational work myself.

## What the Transcripts Reveal

Looking back at today's sessions, the pattern is clear. In the Feedback Control Systems homework, I asked Claude to:
- Complete homework 4 from partial solutions
- Generate transfer functions for PD, PI, and PID controllers
- Create pole-zero plots and MATLAB scripts
- Build an HTML report I could print to PDF

The AI executed everything flawlessly from a technical standpoint. It produced clean HTML with MathJax rendering, generated correct (mostly) transfer function derivations, and even added thoughtful explanations about steady-state error.

But here's what I missed: the transfer functions for problems B.3 and B.4 weren't correct. I only discovered this when I came back later asking Claude to fix them. If I had worked through the block diagram analysis myself, I would have caught this immediately.

## The Motion Control Lab: A Cautionary Tale

The Motion Control sessions were even more revealing. I asked Claude to:
- Complete the pre-lab with all necessary C++ code
- Generate IIR filter implementations
- Create derivative calculation programs for the Sensoray 826 board

Here's a snippet of what got generated:

```cpp
// First Order IIR Filter
// y(n) = 0.832448·y(n-1) + 0.167552·x(n)
double A = 0.832448;
double B = 0.167552;
double y_prev = 0.0;

// In the main loop:
double x_n = (adc_value / 65535.0) * 20.0 - 10.0;  // Convert to voltage
double y_n = A * y_prev + B * x_n;
y_prev = y_n;
```

The code looks correct. The filter coefficients are mathematically sound for a 20 Hz cutoff at 750 Hz sampling. But as my professor pointed out, the load cell code from a previous lab "wasn't anything close to what was expected." I hadn't used the provided template, and the solution was fundamentally incorrect.

The problem wasn't Claude's capability—it was my lack of understanding of what the lab actually required.

## The Response I Wrote

With Claude's help, I drafted a response to my professor:

> "Moving forward from Prelab 4 onward, I will complete all lab reports, code, and calculations entirely on my own without AI assistance. I will follow the lab instructions more carefully and make sure my submissions align with what is expected."

The AI helped me write this email about not using AI. There's poetry in that irony.

## The Real Lesson for Developers

Here's what I'm taking away from today:

1. **AI excels at generation, not validation.** Claude can produce syntactically correct code and mathematically plausible derivations, but it can't verify against the specific expectations of your course, project, or codebase.

2. **Transparency isn't enough.** I thought disclosing AI use was sufficient. It's not. Understanding your own work is what matters.

3. **The "last mile" problem is real.** AI got me 90% of the way there, but that final 10%—where correctness actually matters—required human judgment I hadn't developed.

4. **Context is everything.** Claude didn't know that my professor provided specific code templates. It didn't know the grading rubric or what "correct" looked like for this particular assignment.

## Moving Forward

I'm not abandoning AI tools. They're too valuable for legitimate use cases: understanding error messages, exploring concepts, debugging specific issues. But for learning new material? There's no substitute for struggling through it yourself.

The Advanced Mechanisms project proposal I worked on today will be the last assignment where I lean on AI for the heavy lifting. From here on, the AI becomes a reference tool, not a ghostwriter.

Sometimes the most important lesson from a coding session isn't about code at all—it's about knowing when to close the terminal and pick up a pencil.
